title: IAM, Bucket Policies, Ownership, Access Points, and Public Access · Execution Trace: Access Point Boundary
summary: A bucket is private by convention, one application role has s3:*, tenant prefixes are interpolated from user input, and ACLs conflict with ownership and public-block assumptions. Predict the representation, storage, trust, cost, and recovery evidence before inspecting a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the access point boundary increment in least-privilege S3 authorization proof. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence…
objectives: Use access points and VPC restrictions to separate application or dataset access while retaining bucket-policy and network evidence.
Derive tenant-safe keys and list prefixes server-side and bind allowed actions and conditions without trusting user-supplied policy fragments.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Access Point Boundary · Theory Access Point Boundary
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go file-delivery team handles case 4422 during a regional dependency incident. Build deterministic Go evidence to prove explicit-deny-aware least privilege, public block, ownership, and tenant scope; preserve accessible progress, cancellation, status, and recovery evidence; then change the object version and prove stale authority is rejected. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Use access points and VPC restrictions to separate application or dataset access while retaining bucket-policy and network evidence.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Access Point Boundary contract for the supplied data.
